rooms.h9p1: `POST /api/scheduled` with `{room_id, ciphertext, send_at}`. Node stores in `scheduled_messages`; a cron worker commits on time.
rooms.h9p2: Editable and cancellable until the send_at tick fires. After send, the message becomes an ordinary row in `messages`.

files.title: Files and attachments
files.subtitle: Single-shot for small, resumable for large, client-side encryption for both.
files.intro: Every file upload goes through the same encryption pipeline. The difference between paths is purely about network resilience: resumable tolerates flaky links.
files.h1p1: Cap: 5 MB. Used for photos, voice notes, short clips.
files.h1p2: Client encrypts the whole file with AES-GCM, random 96-bit nonce, key = `file_root = secrets.token_bytes(32)`.
files.h1p3: POSTs `multipart/form-data` to `/api/files` with fields `file` (ciphertext), `plain_blake3` (integrity claim), `mime_type` (hint; server re-checks with `python-magic`).
files.h1p4: Node stores under `uploads/<category>/<sha256>.bin` and returns `{file_id, url}`.
files.h1p5: Client then posts a room message that references the file_id and carries the file_root in its ciphertext so room members can decrypt.
files.h2p2: Stage 1: `POST /api/files/resumable/init` with `{filename, size, chunk_size_hint}`. Node allocates an upload id, picks a chunk size (default 512 KiB, capped at 4 MiB), returns `{upload_id, chunk_size}`.
files.h2p3: Stage 2: for each chunk, `PUT /api/files/resumable/{upload_id}/chunk/{offset}` with raw ciphertext body. Offsets must be aligned to `chunk_size`.
files.h2p4: Stage 3: `POST /api/files/resumable/{upload_id}/finalise` with `{plain_blake3}`. Node reassembles, verifies size, stores, returns `{file_id, url}`.
files.h2p5: Failure handling: interrupted uploads can resume by re-posting only missing offsets. Node tracks which offsets arrived in Redis (or in-memory for SQLite mode).
files.h2p6: TTL: unfinished uploads are purged after 24 h.
files.h3p1: Image uploads (MIME `image/*`) trigger a Pillow-based thumbnail generator at 256×256 JPEG q=80. Stored under `uploads/thumbs/<hash>.jpg`.
files.h3p2: Thumbnails are also client-side encrypted — recipient needs the file_root to render. Saves bandwidth for list previews.
files.h3p3: Video uploads (MIME `video/*`) trigger an ffmpeg-based poster extraction at 10 % of the clip duration, stored the same way.
files.h4p1: Client-submitted `mime_type` is a hint. Server runs `python-magic` against the first 1 KB and rejects if the sniffed type is in the deny list: `application/x-dosexec`, `application/x-sharedlib`, `application/x-mach-binary`.
files.h4p2: This stops naive users from accidentally distributing malware through the messenger. Motivated attackers can still bypass with correctly-labelled containers — the defence is client-side unwrap refusal, not server-side sniff.
files.h5p1: Default: local filesystem. Good for single-node deploys.
files.h5p2: S3: set `STORAGE_BACKEND=s3` + `S3_BUCKET=...`. Files are streamed directly to S3 without touching node's disk. Presigned URLs are used for downloads.
files.h5p3: IPFS: experimental. Uploads announce CIDs through the DHT; downloads fetch any pinning node.

presence.title: Presence and delivery signals
presence.subtitle: Typing indicators, read receipts, last-seen — all ephemeral.
presence.intro: These signals are noisy. Vortex keeps them off-persistence by default: typing lives in Redis (or in-memory), read receipts are the only signal that survives a restart.
presence.h1p1: Client sends `{type:"t", room:..., state:"start"|"stop"}` on keystroke. Debounced to at most one event per 3 s.
presence.h1p2: Node broadcasts `{type:"t", room:..., user:..., state:...}` to all subscribed WebSockets.
presence.h1p3: Auto-expire: `start` without a following `stop` times out after 10 s on the client side.
presence.h2p2: Node persists in `read_receipts(room_id, user_id, message_id, read_at)` and broadcasts `{type:"r", room:..., user:..., message_id:...}`.
presence.h2p3: Clients render "seen by N" under outbound messages.
presence.h2p4: Privacy: user can disable "seen" entirely; then the node doesn't persist receipts for them. Trade-off: they also don't see other people's receipts (symmetry).
presence.h3p1: Node tracks `presence.last_seen_at` (unix ms) per user. Updated on every WebSocket frame.
presence.h3p2: Granularity tiers (user-choosable): exact, rounded to nearest 5 min, rounded to nearest hour, "recently", or off.
presence.h4p1: A user is "online" if they have at least one active WebSocket on any of their devices.
presence.h4p2: Visible to contacts only — non-contacts see last-seen but not a live dot.

calls.title: WebRTC calls
calls.subtitle: Voice + video, 1:1 and group, signalled over WebSocket, relayed via coturn.
calls.intro: Vortex uses the stock WebRTC stack. Node is signalling-only. Once peers connect their ICE candidates, media flows directly (peer-to-peer) or through the TURN relay on bad NAT.
calls.h1p1: Caller: `POST /api/calls/{room_id}/start` with `{video: bool, audio: bool}`. Node allocates a call_id, broadcasts `{type:"c", kind:"invite", call_id, video, audio}` to room.
calls.h1p3: Offer/answer exchange: `{kind:"offer", sdp:...}` → `{kind:"answer", sdp:...}` → `{kind:"candidate", ice:...}` × N until connected.
calls.h1p4: Call ends: `POST /api/calls/{call_id}/end`. Either side can hang up. Node records `call_records(call_id, duration_s, video, audio, parties)`.
calls.h2p1: Server Interactive Connectivity Establishment. Peers exchange candidates (host, srflx, prflx, relay).
calls.h2p2: STUN: bundled with coturn. Stun-only mode used for symmetric-NAT detection.
calls.h2p3: TURN: coturn with HMAC-signed short-lived credentials. Each call gets fresh creds valid for 24 h.
calls.h2p4: Credentials: `{username: <timestamp>:<user_id>, password: base64(HMAC-SHA1(secret, username))}`. Standard RFC 5389 layout.
calls.h3p1: Audio: Opus 48 kHz variable bitrate (6-128 kbps). DTX on for silence suppression.
calls.h3p2: Video: VP9 (preferred) / H.264 / AV1 (opt-in). Simulcast for group calls: caller encodes three resolutions simultaneously.
calls.h3p3: FEC: always on for audio; for video if bitrate headroom allows.
calls.h4p1: For > 2 participants, Vortex spawns an SFU (Selective Forwarding Unit) on the node. Each participant uploads once, SFU fans out.
calls.h4p2: Currently audio-only up to 100 participants; video groups capped at 8 in v0.1 (video SFU hardening in v0.3 roadmap).
calls.h4p3: SFU uses the same coturn relay for traversal; no additional services required.
calls.h5p1: Via `getDisplayMedia`. Client adds a new track to the existing PeerConnection; renegotiates offer/answer.
calls.h5p2: Recipients render the shared screen as a separate video track. On mobile, screen sharing requires OS-level permission — iOS prompts, Android shows a system dialog.
calls.h6p1: WebRTC's built-in NetEQ handles loss and jitter. On severe degradation the client drops resolution or disables video entirely while keeping audio alive.
calls.h6p2: A 500 ms freeze triggers a reconnect attempt (ICE restart). Users see "Reconnecting…" overlay.

federation.title: Federation
federation.subtitle: Trusted pubkeys, signed gossip, cross-node DMs.
federation.intro: Federation lets users on node A message users on node B. It's opt-in per operator. Node pubkeys are explicitly trusted; there is no automatic "any node can federate with mine" mode.
federation.h1p1: Each node maintains `federations(pubkey, endpoint, health_score, added_at, added_by)`.
federation.h1p2: Adding a peer requires operator-signed admin action. The admin UI shows the remote's fingerprint so operators can verify out-of-band.
federation.h1p3: Removed peers remain in DB marked `revoked_at` for audit. New federation requests from the same pubkey are rejected until an admin un-revokes.
federation.h2p1: Outbound from A to user@B: node A wraps the message envelope in a signed blob and POSTs `/federation/deliver` on node B.
federation.h2p2: Envelope: `{source_node, target_user, inner_ciphertext, inner_nonce, sent_at, sig}`. Sig covers everything except sig itself.
federation.h2p3: Node B verifies signature against `source_node` pubkey from its `federations` table. Invalid sig → 401 + audit event.
federation.h3p1: If B is unreachable, A queues in `federation_outbox(target_node, payload, attempts, next_retry_at)`.
federation.h3p2: Exponential backoff: 1s, 5s, 30s, 2min, 10min, 1h, capped at 6h. Permanent failure after 14 days.
federation.h3p3: Operator alert fires when outbox depth > 1000 messages — indicates a stuck federation link.
federation.h4p1: Every 60 s each node pings `GET /v1/health` on each peer. Response time + status tracked.
federation.h4p2: Health score: initial 100. Each timeout subtracts 1; each success adds 1 (cap 100). Score 0 → federation paused for that peer.

gossip.title: Gossip and peer discovery
gossip.subtitle: How nodes find each other without a central registry.
gossip.intro: Beyond the controller's attested list, Vortex nodes gossip peers amongst themselves. This discovers new nodes as operators spin them up, even if the controller hasn't attested them yet.
gossip.h1p1: Every 60 s, node picks a random peer and sends a 256-bit Bloom filter of known peers. Peer replies with the peers NOT in the filter, plus its own filter. Bandwidth is `O(new peers × 32 bytes)`, typically under 1 KiB per round.
gossip.h1p2: Each advertised peer is signed by the advertising node. Clients verify signatures on first contact — an attacker can't forge a peer into the gossip.
gossip.h1p3: The round is rate-limited: one exchange per peer per 30 s (sliding window).
gossip.h2p1: New nodes start with 8 hardcoded bootstrap controllers. First gossip round discovers dozens of peers; the network converges in ~1 minute.
gossip.h3p1: Each advertised peer must be signed by the operator's Ed25519 release key. Forging a peer announcement requires stealing a valid key.
gossip.h3p2: Proof-of-work (adjustable, default 2^18 leading zeros) on new peer registrations keeps bulk Sybil-bot waves impractical.
gossip.h3p3: Reputation system: peers that behave badly (bad signatures, forged announcements, repeated timeouts) have their reputation decay. Zero reputation → blacklist.

stealth.title: Stealth network
stealth.subtitle: Five layers, 28 mechanisms. Everything from TLS padding to Tor onion services.
stealth.intro: Vortex assumes a nation-state-grade adversary. The stealth stack is layered so even when the outer layers are bypassed, inner ones still provide degraded-but-working service.
stealth.h1p1: Layer 1 — fingerprint normalisation. Stops passive classifiers.
stealth.h1p2: Layer 2 — shape / size morphing. Stops traffic-analysis classifiers.
stealth.h1p3: Layer 3 — protocol camouflage. Stops active DPI.
stealth.h1p4: Layer 4 — pluggable transports. Works in networks that block our primary.
stealth.h1p5: Layer 5 — last-resort fallback. Onion, BMP, multi-hop relay. Works when nothing else does.
stealth.h2p1: TLS 1.3 ClientHello matches Chrome on Windows 11 byte-for-byte. JA3/JA4 hash matches Chrome 120 — no Vortex-specific fingerprint.
stealth.h2p2: Record-layer padding: all TLS records padded to 16 KB. Packet sizes match bulk browser traffic.
stealth.h2p3: Constant-rate send: queue emits one frame every N ms regardless of application activity. Idle periods emit dummy frames marked in the inner envelope so recipients discard them.
stealth.h2p4: Timing jitter: 0-50 ms random delay on egress to defeat latency-based DPI.
stealth.h3p1: YouTube-720p morpher: packet-size distribution, burst cadence, and silence gaps match a YouTube live stream. Classifiers looking for messenger heartbeats fail to match.
stealth.h3p2: Multipath: the same flow split over Wi-Fi, cellular, and any active VPN simultaneously. Reassembly at the receiver. Defeats single-path shaping.
stealth.h3p3: WebRTC data channel tunnel: when TLS is blocked but WebRTC works, Vortex tunnels through a DTLS-SRTP channel to a known peer.
stealth.h3p4: Decoy connections: 3 long-lived idle TLS sessions to Google, YouTube, and Cloudflare. Observer sees normal browser behaviour.
stealth.h4p1: DoH (RFC 8484): all DNS resolved via DoH to Cloudflare / Google / Quad9. ISP sees only HTTPS to those resolvers, not the actual domain list.
stealth.h4p2: ECH (Encrypted Client Hello): SNI encrypted with a key published in the DNS HTTPS record. Passive observers can't see the destination hostname.
stealth.h4p3: Probe detection: node tracks requests with no auth session. Three failed probes from an IP in 5 min trigger the "decoy site" mode — that IP now sees a plain HTML landing page with 200 OK.
stealth.h4p4: DGA (Domain Generation Algorithm): deterministic domain generator seeded with today's date. If primary endpoint is blocked, clients compute the next candidate and try.
stealth.h5p1: vmess / vless / trojan / shadowtls: standard V2Ray-style transports with a pluggable JSON config.
stealth.h5p2: Reality: TLS-within-TLS tunnel. Outer handshake targets www.microsoft.com; inner handshake is Vortex. Probes against the outer get forwarded to Microsoft so active probing reveals nothing.
stealth.h5p3: Snowflake: volunteer browsers relay Vortex traffic over WebRTC. Users installing the Snowflake extension provide cover.
stealth.h5p4: NaiveProxy: modifies HTTPS probes to blend with Chromium's own behaviour down to the microsecond.
stealth.h6p1: Onion services: every node also exposes itself as `.onion`. Clients with Tor support fall through to this when everything else fails.
stealth.h6p2: Blind Mailbox Protocol (BMP): anonymous store-and-forward for messages. See BMP chapter.
stealth.h6p3: Multi-hop relay: messages can bounce through up to 5 intermediate nodes, each adding a layer of envelope. Like Tor but in our own mesh.
stealth.h6p4: OHTTP (RFC 9458): oblivious HTTP through a relay. No single party sees both request and response.

bmp.title: Blind Mailbox Protocol
bmp.subtitle: Anonymous store-and-forward for messages through the gossip mesh.
bmp.intro: BMP lets a sender deposit a message at any node without revealing identity. The message gossips until it reaches the recipient's home node, where it decrypts as usual.
bmp.h1p1: Sender derives `mailbox_id = HKDF(recipient_pubkey, info: "bmp-v1", len: 32)`. Only sender (who knows recipient_pubkey) can compute it; recipient also knows.
bmp.h1p2: Sender posts `POST /bmp/deposit` with `{mailbox_id, blob}` to any gossiping node.
bmp.h1p3: Node stores pair for up to 7200 s. Node also includes the mailbox_id in next gossip round's Bloom filter.
bmp.h1p4: Other nodes pull blobs for mailbox_ids they locally care about (recipients on their node).
bmp.h1p5: Recipient's client polls `GET /bmp/messages` and decrypts any new blobs with its usual key material.
bmp.h2p1: No node can link a blob to a sender — the sender's TLS connection identifies the source IP, but a chain of onion hops between sender and first BMP node removes that too.
bmp.h2p2: Recipient-hiding: blob is encrypted with recipient's pubkey. Middle nodes see only an opaque byte string and a random mailbox_id.
bmp.h2p3: No delivery confirmation to sender. At-most-once semantics.
bmp.h3p1: Max blob size: 1 MB. Larger payloads must be chunked through the ordinary file flow.
bmp.h3p2: Max storage per node: 500 MB of deposits in total. LRU eviction at cap. Operators can tune.
bmp.h3p3: Spam: per-IP deposit rate limited to 10 / min. Mailbox IDs observed more than 3 times / hour on one node are rate-limited as suspected flood.
bmp.h4p1: High-censorship recipients: sender doesn't need to know recipient's home node.
bmp.h4p2: Intermittent connectivity: blobs wait until recipient re-connects and polls.
bmp.h4p3: Anonymous whistleblowing: sender-side onion + BMP = no metadata on who deposited what.

push.title: Push notifications
push.subtitle: APNs, FCM, Web Push — all with sealed envelopes so providers can't read.
push.intro: Standard push services (APNs, FCM, Web Push) see every payload. Vortex wraps each notification in a separate E2E layer so the provider sees only an opaque blob with no room id, sender, or text.
push.h1p1: Client obtains a platform token (APNs device token, FCM registration id, or Web Push subscription JWT).
push.h1p2: Client generates a fresh 32-byte X25519 key `p256dh_pub` and a 16-byte `auth` secret. POSTs `/api/push/subscribe` with `{endpoint, p256dh, auth, platform}`.
push.h1p3: Node persists `push_subscriptions(user_id, endpoint, p256dh, auth, platform, created_at)`.
push.h2p1: When a notification is due (new message in a muted room that's set to notify, mention, call invite), node encrypts the payload with AES-GCM using a key derived from `ECDH(p256dh_pub, ephemeral_priv)` via HKDF.
push.h2p2: Node packages `{endpoint, TTL, urgency, topic: <collapse_key>, data: <ciphertext>}` and sends to APNs / FCM / Web Push server.
push.h2p3: Provider sees only `data` (ciphertext) + `topic`. No plaintext leak.
push.h3p1: Per-room topic key: `room:<id>`. Multiple messages in the same room collapse to one notification at the OS level.
push.h3p2: Per-call topic: `call:<id>`. Answering or declining pulls the banner; new call invites to the same room replace the old one.
push.h4p1: iOS: Notification Service Extension decrypts in the NSE process (separate from main app). Uses the same CryptoKit primitives.
push.h4p2: Android: FirebaseMessagingService decrypts in a background service. JobScheduler schedules actual UI update when app is foregrounded.
push.h4p3: Web: Service Worker decrypts in the SW context, posts a local notification via Web Notifications API.
push.h5p1: APNs: requires provider token refreshed every 60 min. Token refresh is automated via Apple's `auth-team-id` + `auth-key-id` + p8 key file.
push.h5p2: FCM: legacy HTTP endpoint deprecated; we use the HTTP v1 endpoint with OAuth2 service account credentials. Rotation every 1 h.
push.h5p3: Web Push: VAPID keys (one keypair per node). TTL capped at 28 days by most browsers.

controller.title: Controller service
controller.subtitle: Integrity attestation, entry discovery, health monitoring.
controller.intro: The controller is a separate Python process. Its attack surface is tiny (about a dozen routes) and its trust is pinned to a single Ed25519 public key baked into clients.
controller.h1p1: `GET /v1/integrity` — attestation result. Schema: `{status, signed_by, trusted_pubkey, version, built_at, matched, mismatched, missing, extra, message}`.
controller.h1p2: `GET /v1/health` — liveness. Schema: `{status: "ok", version, pubkey, stats:{online, approved, total}}`.
controller.h1p6: `GET /v1/heartbeat/{pubkey}` — node liveness probe. Used by federated nodes to keep their registration fresh.
controller.h1p7: `POST /v1/register` — a new node applies for trust. Requires admin approval unless `AUTO_APPROVE=true`.
controller.h2p1: Manifest file: `INTEGRITY.sig.json`. Contains per-file BLAKE3 hash + Ed25519 signature.
controller.h2p2: Generation: `python -m vortex_controller.integrity.sign_tool`. Reads the release key from `keys/release.key`, walks tree, writes manifest.
controller.h2p3: Verification: controller reads the manifest at startup and compares against disk. Startup fails loudly on mismatch.
controller.h3p1: Mirrors run the same controller code with the same release key copied over. Clients see identical `signed_by` across mirrors — one way to detect a fake mirror is to compare pubkeys.
controller.h3p2: DNSLink / SNS / IPFS publish pointers to one of several mirrors. Clients try in priority order with timeouts.
controller.h3p3: The `vortex.sol-mirror/` directory in this repo is a ready-to-go trycloudflare mirror for development.

storage.title: Storage layer
storage.subtitle: SQLite for dev, Postgres for prod, Alembic for migrations, GRDB/Room/IndexedDB on clients.
storage.intro: Vortex's node storage is SQL. Switching between SQLite (dev) and Postgres (prod) is a DATABASE_URL change — no code forks.
storage.h1p1: `users(id, username, password_hash, x25519_public_key, kyber_public_key?, display_name, avatar_url, phone, email, created_at, is_active)` — primary identity table.
storage.h1p2: `user_devices(id, user_id, device_id, user_agent, last_seen_at, revoked_at)` — per-device session rows.
storage.h1p3: `rooms(id, type, name, is_private, is_channel, is_dm, member_count, avatar_url, created_at, created_by, description)` — room table, one row per room.
storage.h1p4: `room_members(room_id, user_id, role, joined_at, muted_until)` — membership + ACL.
storage.h1p5: `messages(id, room_id, sender_id, ciphertext, nonce, sender_pseudo, sent_at, edited_at?, deleted_at?, reply_to?, thread_id?, kind)` — every message, indexed on `(room_id, id)`.
storage.h1p6: `reactions(message_id, user_id, emoji, created_at)` — compound primary key `(message_id, user_id, emoji)`.
storage.h1p7: `files(id, user_id, mime_type, size, sha256 /* actually BLAKE3 */, storage_path, uploaded_at)` — file metadata.
storage.h1p8: `read_receipts(room_id, user_id, message_id, read_at)` — latest-read pointer per user per room.
storage.h1p9: `presence(user_id, last_seen_at, is_typing_in_room_id?)` — ephemeral, Redis-backed in prod.
storage.h1p10: `federations(pubkey, endpoint, health_score, added_at, added_by, revoked_at)` — peer list.
storage.h1p11: `federation_outbox(id, target_node, payload, attempts, next_retry_at)` — outbound queue.
storage.h1p12: `scheduled_messages(id, room_id, ciphertext, nonce, send_at, created_by, created_at, cancelled_at)`.
storage.h1p14: `saved_gifs(user_id, url, width, height, added_at)` — per-user GIF collection.
storage.h1p15: `folders(user_id, id, name, room_ids, is_system, created_at)` — chat folders.
storage.h1p17: `sessions(jti, user_id, device_id, expires_at)` — refresh token registry.
storage.h1p18: `push_subscriptions(user_id, endpoint, p256dh, auth, platform, created_at)` — push registration.
storage.h2p1: Alembic in `alembic/versions/`. Linear history, one revision per release (sometimes a few per release).
storage.h2p2: Dev mode: `create_all + ALTER TABLE fallback` for rapid iteration.
storage.h2p3: Prod mode: `alembic upgrade head` before startup. Docker image runs it in the entrypoint.
storage.h3p1: `messages(room_id, id DESC)` — primary index for paginated fetch.
storage.h3p2: `messages(room_id, sent_at DESC)` — for time-based ordering on edit.
storage.h3p6: FTS virtual tables (FTS5 SQLite / tsvector Postgres) for full-text search.
storage.h4p1: `make db-backup` produces a timestamped archive. Encrypted at rest with operator key.
storage.h4p2: Retention: daily for 14 days, weekly for 4 weeks, monthly for 12 months.
storage.h4p3: Restoration: `make db-restore FILE=<path>`. Runs on a staging DB first; operator verifies then swaps.

bots.title: Bot framework
bots.subtitle: Gravitix DSL + Python SDK + marketplace + antispam.
bots.intro: Bots are first-class room participants. Messages are signed with a bot's Ed25519 key so forgeries are detectable.
bots.h1p1: Two options: Python SDK or Gravitix DSL. Python is for complex workflows; Gravitix for event-driven chat bots that need to ship quickly.
bots.h1p2:
Python example: `from vortex_bot import Bot
bot = Bot(token=...)
@bot.on_message
async def echo(msg): await bot.send(msg.room_id, msg.text)
bot.run()`. The SDK handles WebSocket, reconnection, encryption key exchange.
bots.h1p3: Gravitix example: `on /start { emit "Hello, {ctx.first_name}!" }`. Compiles to bytecode and runs in a sandboxed interpreter.
bots.h1p4: Bot token is issued at bot creation. Lost token? `POST /api/bots/{id}/rotate-token` issues a new one.
bots.h2p1: Two deployment models: in-process (coroutine inside the node) and external (bot polls `/api/bots/{id}/updates`).
bots.h2p2: In-process: zero network hop, fastest, but operator must trust bot code. Good for own bots.
bots.h2p3: External: webhook or long-poll. No node-level trust needed. Good for third-party bots and the marketplace.
bots.h2p4: Gravitix bots always run in-process in the sandbox — they have no filesystem or network access except explicit API.
bots.h3p1: Public bots can submit to the marketplace. Reviewers verify code doesn't phish, doesn't spam, doesn't leak user data.
bots.h3p2: Categories: productivity, games, news, utilities, ai, translation.
bots.h3p3: Install: one click. Node subscribes the user to the bot's username so DMs route correctly.
bots.h3p4: Monetization: free, one-time purchase, subscription. Platform fee 10 %% of gross.
bots.h4p1: Every room is monitored by an invisible antispam bot. Checks: link density > 3 per minute, capital-letter ratio > 70 %% for 10+ chars, repeat messages, sudden burst of new-account joiners.
bots.h4p2: Actions: warn → timeout (5 min) → kick → ban. Appeals go to room admin.

ops.title: Operations
ops.subtitle: Deploy, monitor, rollback.
ops.intro: Every operational task has a `make` target or a CLI command. Ops pages live in `deploy/`.
ops.h1p1: `docker compose up` brings up node, controller, Postgres, Redis, coturn, Caddy.
ops.h1p2: Volumes: `/data/vortex.db`, `/data/uploads`, `/data/logs`. Mount to host for persistence.
ops.h1p3: Environment: `.env` in repo root. Never commit. `.env.example` is the template.
ops.h1p4: Caddy auto-fetches Let's Encrypt certs. Let's Encrypt rate limits apply — use staging for experiments.
ops.h2p1: `make install` — install Python deps + dev tools.
ops.h2p2: `make dev` — uvicorn with reload on port 9000.
ops.h2p4: `make lint` — ruff + mypy. CI blocks on failures.
ops.h2p8: `make ci` — run the full CI pipeline locally.
ops.h3p1: `Vortex Wizard.app` is a PyInstaller bundle with a pywebview GUI.
ops.h3p2: First-run flow: choose node name → generate release keys → configure TLS (Let's Encrypt / bring-your-own / self-signed) → pick entry URLs → enable stealth → open firewall ports.
ops.h3p3: After setup, wizard stays as the admin console: start/stop node, view logs, rotate keys, add federated peers.
ops.h4p1: Every release tagged in git. Tag also signed with release key so the tag itself is verifiable.
ops.h4p2: Rollback: `git checkout <tag> && make docker-build && docker compose up -d`. Running nodes drain and restart.
ops.h4p3: DB: Alembic `downgrade <rev>` supported for one minor version back. Further back requires manual intervention.

mobile.title: Mobile clients
mobile.subtitle: iOS and Android — native UI, same protocol.
mobile.intro: Both mobile clients are full peers, not thin web wrappers. Native look, native performance, native integrations (CallKit, ConnectionService, Lock Screen widgets).
mobile.h1p1: Swift Package in `ios/Modules/`. 30+ feature modules, each SOLID-split `api/` + `impl/` + `ui/`.
mobile.h1p2: App target in `ios/VortexApp/`. XcodeGen spec at `ios/project.yml` regenerates the xcodeproj.
mobile.h1p3: Crypto: CryptoKit (X25519, Ed25519, AES-GCM, HKDF) + Argon2Swift (Argon2id).
mobile.h1p4: DB: GRDB + FTS5. All mutations go through `db.write`.
mobile.h1p6: Push: APNs via `UNUserNotificationCenter` + Notification Service Extension for decryption.
mobile.h2p1: Gradle project in `android/`. 22 feature modules with Hilt DI, same `api / impl / di / ui` split.
mobile.h3p1: Same 146-locale bundle with native names (`Русский ru`, `English en`, `中文 zh`, …).
mobile.h3p2: Same 1500-emoji catalog with 9 categories + skin-tones + MRU + search.
mobile.h3p3: Same multi-account X25519 challenge-response for switching.
mobile.h3p4: Same chat folders (All / Archived / custom) with drag-and-drop reordering.
mobile.h3p5: Same saved-GIFs list (no Tenor — personal collection only).
mobile.h3p6: Same scheduled-messages UI, contacts screen, premium status page.

webclient.title: Web client
webclient.subtitle: PWA, vanilla JS, offline mode, service worker.
webclient.intro: Web client is a vanilla-JS PWA. No React, no bundler beyond ESM. ~400 KB gzipped total, including 146 locales.
webclient.h1p1: `static/js/main.js` is the entry. Feature modules lazy-load on first use.
webclient.h1p2: State: per-room ring buffers in IndexedDB, in-memory decrypted cache. Service Worker mediates fetch.
webclient.h1p3: UI: Liquid Glass effects via CSS backdrop-filter. No framework; event delegation + idempotent render helpers.
webclient.h2p1: Any write made while offline queues to IndexedDB. SW flushes when connection returns.
webclient.h2p2: Idempotency key on every POST lets duplicate retries succeed safely.
webclient.h2p3: Reading works fully offline — all synced ciphertext lives in IndexedDB.
webclient.h3p1: 146 locales in `static/locales/*.json`. Welcome screen (`lang-picker.js`) cycles through native-name hints.
webclient.h3p2: Runtime locale switch: `document.documentElement.lang = code` + re-render labels via `data-i18n` attributes.

security.title: Security posture
security.subtitle: Threat model, defence-in-depth, WAF, audit trail, canary.
security.intro: Vortex uses defence-in-depth: breaking one layer isn't enough, a realistic attacker needs multiple independent wins.
security.h1p1: **Passive network observer**: sees TLS packets; does size/timing analysis. Defeated by Level 1 stealth.
security.h1p2: **Active network attacker**: can drop/delay/inject. Defeated by Level 2-4 stealth.
security.h1p3: **Compromised node operator**: sees ciphertext + metadata. Message content remains confidential.
security.h1p4: **Stolen client device**: plaintext of past messages exposed. Double Ratchet limits to current session; wipe-on-next-unlock available.
security.h1p5: **Quantum adversary with stored past traffic**: defeated by hybrid Kyber-768 session keys when both peers advertised PQ.
security.h2p1: Request inspector: SQLi patterns, path traversal, template injection, LFI, NoSQLi. Drops before app code.
security.h2p2: Rate limits: global 1000 req/s soft cap with burst; per-route (login 10/min, register 5/min); per-user 100 req/s after auth.
security.h3p1: Security-relevant events (admin action, federation change, release upgrade, panic wipe) hash-chained via `app/security/canary.py`.
security.h3p2: Chain break ⇒ tampering detected. Alert fires.
security.h3p3: `/api/admin/audit` exposes the log (admin auth only). Append-only.
security.h4p1: Canary file signed daily with release key. Absence ⇒ compromise suspected.
security.h4p2: Emergency revocation: admin broadcasts `{type:"n", event:"compromise", ...}` to every WebSocket. Clients wipe cached keys.
security.h4p3: Rotation: new release key, re-sign manifest, re-publish. Clients re-bootstrap.

privacy.title: Privacy and compliance
privacy.subtitle: GDPR, right-to-erasure, metadata minimisation, data portability.
privacy.intro: Vortex ships GDPR and CCPA tooling out of the box. Operators in regulated jurisdictions have a single admin endpoint for every statutory request.
privacy.h1p1: No IP logs by default. Reverse proxy strips `X-Real-IP` unless operator opts in.
privacy.h1p2: `sender_pseudo` in message rows hashes real sender IDs — a DB dump reveals only per-room pseudonyms.
privacy.h1p3: Search uses encrypted-tokens. Client encrypts keywords with room key; node stores opaque tokens; lookup is exact-match on ciphertext tokens.
privacy.h1p4: Last-seen truncated to user's chosen granularity.
privacy.h2p1: `POST /api/privacy/erase` anonymises the user row, wipes their sent messages (metadata retained for quorum), deletes files from disk.
privacy.h2p2: Federation: signed gossip informs peers; they erase on their side within 72 h.
privacy.h2p3: Audit: erasure is logged with the timestamp and (hashed) user id so operator can prove compliance.
privacy.h3p1: `GET /api/privacy/export` streams a zip: profile JSON, sent/received ciphertext, file references with per-file root keys, contact list.
privacy.h3p2: Generation time: ~5 min for 10 k messages, bounded by IO.
privacy.h3p3: Recipient must decrypt ciphertext locally; export tooling is shipped alongside.
privacy.h4p1: Long-press logo 5 s → immediate local wipe: keys, cached messages, attachments.
privacy.h4p2: Coupled wipe: `/api/privacy/panic` also tombstones the server-side account.

debugging.title: Debugging and diagnostics
debugging.subtitle: Tools for tracking down issues in dev, staging, and prod.
debugging.intro: Every subsystem has structured logging and a /metrics surface. Here's the usual diagnosis playbook.
debugging.h1p2: Rotate at 100 MB; keep 14 gens gzipped.
debugging.h1p4: Correlation id: every request gets an `X-Request-Id`; downstream RPCs reuse it.
debugging.h2p2: RED: rate / errors / duration per route.
debugging.h2p3: USE: utilisation / saturation / errors per DB pool, WebSocket fan-out, TURN relay.
debugging.h2p4: Custom: active rooms, messages/s, stealth-level histogram.
debugging.h3p1: `py-spy` for runtime sampling. Attach to the running node PID.
debugging.h3p2: `memray` for heap profiling. 1-minute snapshot reveals leaks quickly.
debugging.h3p3: Rust extension `vortex_chat` exports `--profile` flag that writes a flamegraph-compatible trace.
debugging.h4p1: `scripts/smoke/all.sh` runs register/login/send/receive/logout against a local node. Exits non-zero on any failure.
debugging.h4p2: CI runs smoke on every PR as well as on merges to main.
debugging.h4p3: Production smoke: external probe hitting a canary account once per minute.

testing.title: Testing strategy
testing.subtitle: 29 k lines of tests across Python, Swift, Kotlin, JS.
testing.intro: Every layer has its own test suite. CI runs all of them in under 10 minutes.
testing.h1p1: `pytest app/tests` covers routers, security, transport, bots.
testing.h1p2: Hypothesis property-based tests on crypto: `assert decrypt(encrypt(m, k), k) == m` for 1000 random m.
testing.h1p3: AFL-style fuzz on WAF: millions of malformed inputs — asserts no 5xx leaks.
testing.h2p1: `swift test` in `ios/Modules/` runs unit tests against RFC 7748 / 8032 / 5869 / 8446 test vectors.
testing.h2p2: XCUI tests drive SwiftUI views in a headless simulator; snapshot-compared.
testing.h3p2: `./gradlew connectedCheck` runs instrumented tests on a running emulator.
testing.h4p1: Playwright drives the web client, headless Chrome + headless Firefox. Calls are exercised with `--use-fake-ui-for-media-stream`.
testing.h4p2: Scenarios: register two accounts → DM → send file → delete → verify sync → end.

apiSurface.title: API surface
apiSurface.subtitle: Every public HTTP route with verb, auth requirement, and purpose.
apiSurface.intro: Complete listing. Paths relative to node base URL. Auth column: `none` (pre-auth), `jwt` (Bearer access token), `refresh` (Bearer refresh token), `admin` (admin JWT).
apiSurface.h1p21: `POST /api/authentication/qr/verify` — none — accept QR on new device.
apiSurface.h9p2: `GET /bmp/messages` — jwt — pull blobs addressed to user's mailbox.

roadmap.title: Roadmap
roadmap.subtitle: What's planned and when.
roadmap.intro: High-level view. Dates are aspirational; security issues pre-empt feature work.
roadmap.h1p1: Kyber-768 PQ enabled by default on fresh installs.
roadmap.h1p2: Groups up to 50 000 members with Variant-B key publish.
roadmap.h1p3: Desktop (Tauri) packaging for macOS / Windows / Linux.
roadmap.h1p4: Bot marketplace opens to public submissions.
roadmap.h2p1: Audio-only group calls up to 100 participants via SFU.
roadmap.h2p3: Per-participant mute / kick in the call UI.
roadmap.h3p1: Miro-style collaborative canvas over CRDT (Yjs-flavoured) through the messaging layer.
roadmap.h4p1: On-chain escrow for bot purchases (Solana + EVM).
roadmap.h4p3: Fiat on/off ramp via Stripe connect.
roadmap.h5p1: Above stabilised, formal security audit, reproducible builds, SemVer frozen.
roadmap.h5p3: At least one independent security review with public write-up.

glossary.title: Glossary
glossary.subtitle: Terms you'll see across the docs.
glossary.g1d: Authenticated encryption mode. 256-bit key, 96-bit nonce, 128-bit tag.
glossary.g2d: Database migration tool for SQLAlchemy. Linear revision history.
glossary.g3d: Apple Push Notification service. Used for iOS push.
glossary.g4d: Memory-hard password hash. Vortex params: m=64MiB, t=3, p=4.
glossary.g5d: Additional Authenticated Data. In AES-GCM, data included in the tag but not encrypted.
glossary.g6d: Fast cryptographic hash. Vortex uses it for file integrity; column is still named sha256 for migration compatibility.
glossary.g7d: Blind Mailbox Protocol. Store-and-forward through the gossip mesh.
glossary.g8d: Signed daily file proving the release key still controls the deployment.
glossary.g9d: Concise Binary Object Representation. RFC 8949. Encoding used for E2E message payloads.
glossary.g10d: Vortex's separate attestation process. Publishes `/v1/integrity` and entry URLs.
glossary.g12d: Domain Generation Algorithm. Deterministic fallback domain generator.
glossary.g13d: Encrypted Client Hello. Hides SNI on TLS 1.3.
glossary.g14d: EdDSA signature scheme over edwards25519. 32-byte pubkey, 64-byte signature.
glossary.g15d: The wrapper around ciphertext carrying nonce + metadata.
glossary.g16d: Firebase Cloud Messaging. Used for Android push.
glossary.g17d: Nodes trusting each other to deliver messages to users on other nodes.
glossary.g20d: Vortex's domain-specific language for writing bots.
glossary.g21d: HMAC-based Key Derivation Function. RFC 5869. SHA-256 variant used.
glossary.g23d: Interactive Connectivity Establishment. WebRTC's NAT traversal.
glossary.g24d: JSON Web Token. Vortex uses HS256-signed JWTs for session auth.
glossary.g26d: A recipient's inbox for BMP blobs. ID derived from recipient's pubkey.
glossary.g27d: Number used once. 96-bit random value for AES-GCM.
glossary.g28d: A Python process running the Vortex back-end. Stores ciphertext + fans out events.
glossary.g29d: Oblivious HTTP. RFC 9458. Relay-assisted anonymous HTTP.
glossary.g30d: Tor hidden service. Every Vortex node exposes itself as .onion.
glossary.g31d: Ephemeral pubkey published ahead of time to bootstrap new DMs.
glossary.g32d: One step in the Double Ratchet chain. Each message ratchets forward.
glossary.g33d: Stealth transport that masquerades as a real big-tech site.
glossary.g34d: 32-byte symmetric key shared among room members. Rotated on membership change.
glossary.g35d: Push payload encrypted so the provider (Apple/Google) can't read it.
glossary.g36d: Selective Forwarding Unit. Mixes media in group calls.
glossary.g37d: Tor transport using volunteer browsers as WebRTC relays.
glossary.g38d: RFC 8446. Transport used by every HTTPS and WSS connection.
glossary.g40d: Traversal Using Relays around NAT. Coturn handles it in Vortex.
glossary.g41d: RFC 8292. Voluntary Application Server Identification for Web Push.
glossary.g42d: Web Application Firewall. Vortex ships a built-in one.

websocketFanout.title: WebSocket fan-out internals
websocketFanout.subtitle: How one node delivers an event to thousands of subscribers.
websocketFanout.what: The WebSocket fan-out is a Python-native set of subscriber handles per room. When a message is POSTed, the fan-out dispatcher iterates the set and enqueues a serialised frame onto each subscriber's send queue.
websocketFanout.why: Fan-out avoids the N-copy cost of broadcasting: one decryption on the sender side, one serialised frame, N send-queue pushes. Each push is O(1); the copy cost is pushed onto the kernel at `send()` time via zero-copy buffers when the OS supports them.
websocketFanout.where: Implemented in `app/chats/fanout.py`. The top-level `FanoutRegistry` holds `dict[room_id, set[WebSocket]]`. Registry writes are guarded by `asyncio.Lock`; reads are lock-free snapshots.
websocketFanout.how: On message POST the router calls `await fanout.broadcast(room_id, payload_bytes)`. The method copies the set under the lock, releases, then iterates the copy awaiting `ws.send_bytes(payload)` with a 1-second timeout per socket. Timed-out sockets are marked bad and purged.
websocketFanout.when: Every message, reaction, read-receipt, typing indicator, call signal, and room key rotation flows through this same fan-out. Frame size is bounded at 64 KiB; larger payloads are rejected and returned to the caller via HTTP.
websocketFanout.cfg1: `FANOUT_QUEUE_SIZE=256` — per-subscriber send queue depth. Overflow drops the oldest frame.
websocketFanout.cfg2: `FANOUT_SEND_TIMEOUT_MS=1000` — upper bound on `send_bytes`. Exceeding marks the socket bad.
websocketFanout.cfg3: `FANOUT_MAX_ROOM_SIZE=10000` — refuses fan-out on rooms larger than this. Huge rooms use channel mode and a pull pattern instead.
websocketFanout.fail1: Slow consumer — per-socket queue overflows. Oldest frames dropped. Client catches up via `since` on reconnect.
websocketFanout.fail2: Dead socket — `send_bytes` raises `ConnectionClosed`. Dispatcher removes from set and moves on.
websocketFanout.fail3: Bursty producer — if producer is > 5× faster than aggregate consumer bandwidth, back-pressure kicks in via `await` suspending the sender.
websocketFanout.fail4: Memory blow-up — per-subscriber queue capped at 256 frames; at 64 KiB each that's 16 MiB max per slow consumer. A runaway consumer will get disconnected by the liveness check before hitting that ceiling.
websocketFanout.mon2: Counter `vortex_fanout_drops_total{reason}` — dropped frames by reason (overflow, timeout, closed).
websocketFanout.tune1: Raise `FANOUT_QUEUE_SIZE` for clients on slow-but-stable networks.
websocketFanout.tune2: Lower `FANOUT_SEND_TIMEOUT_MS` in high-contention clusters where slow sockets starve others.
websocketFanout.tune3: Split huge rooms into sub-rooms or switch them to channel mode with pull-based updates.
websocketFanout.edge1: Room rotation events (`type:"k"`) are sent with a 2 s timeout instead of 1 s because clients must re-import keys before accepting more messages.
websocketFanout.edge2: Typing events are not retried — losing a "user stopped typing" is harmless.
websocketFanout.edge3: Presence updates are batched: multiple user presence flips in the same 250 ms window are coalesced into one frame.
websocketFanout.mig1: v0.0 used per-room `asyncio.Queue`s — replaced in v0.0.5 by the lock-copy-iterate pattern for lower per-message CPU.
websocketFanout.mig2: Future: when Redis is available, fan-out will prefer pub/sub so multi-node clusters replicate events without mesh fan-out.
websocketFanout.ts1: High drop rate: check network to the slow consumer; bump queue size; investigate client behaviour.
websocketFanout.ts2: High send duration: likely a TLS renegotiation in progress or a NAT traversal timeout; check kernel logs for TCP retransmissions.
websocketFanout.ts3: Memory creep: inspect `vortex_fanout_queue_depth` per user; mark any user with persistent depth > 200 as a likely slow or malicious client.
websocketFanout.faq1: Q: What happens if a client misses frames during a disconnect? A: It reconnects with `since=<last_seen_id>` and the server replays from the DB. The fan-out is best-effort real-time; durability lives in the DB.
websocketFanout.faq2: Q: Can a malicious subscriber slow down fan-out? A: They slow down their own queue, which fills, drops frames, and disconnects them. No cross-subscriber interference.
websocketFanout.faq3: Q: How do I debug a specific user's delivery? A: `GET /api/admin/debug/fanout/{user_id}` dumps their queue depth, last send timestamp, and recent drops.

nonceManagement.title: Nonce management
nonceManagement.subtitle: How Vortex keeps AES-GCM nonces unique across devices, rooms, and reboots.
nonceManagement.what: Every AES-GCM operation takes a 12-byte nonce that MUST be unique for a given key. Re-using a nonce with the same key destroys both confidentiality and authenticity — an attacker with two ciphertexts under the same (key, nonce) can recover the plaintext XOR.
nonceManagement.why: Vortex generates one room key and uses it for thousands of messages. A compromised nonce across two messages would leak plaintext. All nonces are therefore generated with the OS CSPRNG at 96 bits — the birthday-bound-safe region is about 2^32 messages per key, far more than any single key will encrypt.
nonceManagement.where: Client-side nonce generation lives in `VortexCrypto` (iOS), `crypto/aead` module (Android), `app.security.crypto.aes_gcm_encrypt` (node). The node also runs a defensive duplicate-nonce check.
nonceManagement.how: Step-by-step: client generates 12 random bytes, passes to AES-GCM encrypt, frames the ciphertext as `{ciphertext, nonce}` and POSTs. Node records `(room_id, nonce)` in an in-memory LRU cache sized to 10^6 entries. On POST, node checks the cache — duplicate ⇒ 400 `duplicate_nonce`. Under normal usage this never fires (P(collision) at 10^9 messages is ~10^-19).
nonceManagement.when: On every send. Receiver-side: no nonce check — AES-GCM's tag handles integrity. If a nonce somehow repeated, the second decrypt would still succeed but confidentiality would be compromised.
nonceManagement.cfg1: `NONCE_CACHE_SIZE=1000000` — number of recent nonces tracked per node.
nonceManagement.cfg2: `NONCE_CACHE_TTL_S=86400` — older entries evicted after 24 h (most keys rotate within hours anyway).
nonceManagement.fail1: RNG failure on client — extremely unlikely with OS-provided CSPRNG. We never fall back to non-cryptographic RNGs.
nonceManagement.fail2: Cache miss on node restart — acceptable because cache is defence-in-depth; clients never legitimately reuse nonces so a miss doesn't open an attack window.
nonceManagement.mon2: Gauge `vortex_nonce_cache_size` — should be steady around cache cap.
nonceManagement.tune1: Huge rooms: increase cache size to cover longer rotation windows.
nonceManagement.tune2: Multi-node clusters: share the cache through Redis when available.
nonceManagement.edge1: Offline client re-connecting with queued messages: each queued message carries its own random nonce. No coordination needed.
nonceManagement.edge2: Multi-device send: both devices generate independent random nonces. P(collision) at 2 devices × 10^4 messages = negligible.
nonceManagement.mig1: v0.0 briefly used counter-based nonces seeded with device id — deprecated after a paper showed cross-device collisions under clock-skew. Random-96 is now the only blessed path.
nonceManagement.ts1: See `duplicate_nonce` rejections: inspect the client for buggy RNG; check device entropy source.
nonceManagement.ts2: Cache OOM: check cache size against room write rate; either raise size or lower TTL.
nonceManagement.faq1: Q: What if two clients pick the same nonce by chance? A: At 96 bits of randomness, expected first collision across all users is at ~2^48 messages — many lifetimes of Vortex usage. If it ever happened, the node would reject the second one.
nonceManagement.faq2: Q: Why 96 bits and not 128? A: AES-GCM's standard nonce length. 96 bits also saves 4 bytes per message.

keyRotation.title: Key rotation
keyRotation.subtitle: When and how room, device, and release keys roll over.
keyRotation.what: Every long-lived key in Vortex rotates on a schedule or on trigger. Room keys rotate on membership change or periodically (weekly). Device keys rotate per user choice. Release keys rotate when a compromise is suspected.
keyRotation.why: Rotation limits the blast radius of a compromise. A stolen room key from yesterday stops decrypting today's messages. A stolen release key from 2024 stops attesting to 2026 releases.
keyRotation.where: `app/keys/` houses server-side rotation logic. Client side: `ios/.../Keys/impl/RoomKeyProvider.swift`, `android/.../keys/impl/RoomKeyProvider.kt`.
keyRotation.how: Room rotation: any admin can trigger; ordinary rotation on leave is automatic. The rotator derives a fresh 32-byte root, re-envelopes to remaining members, posts `POST /api/rooms/{id}/rotate` with the new envelopes. Node atomically updates `room_keys` and broadcasts `{type:"k"}`.
keyRotation.when: Room: on leave, on admin trigger, every 7 days automatically. Device: every 90 days by default (configurable). Release: ad-hoc on suspected compromise.
keyRotation.cfg3: `ROTATE_ON_LEAVE=true` — turn off to skip rotation on leave (lowers security for lower CPU).
keyRotation.fail1: Client offline during rotation: catches up on next connect. Node stores last 5 rotations so reconnects always find their envelope.
keyRotation.fail2: Envelope minting race: two admins rotate simultaneously. Node accepts the first and returns 409 Conflict to the second.
keyRotation.tune1: Public rooms at > 1000 members: switch to Variant-B key publish. Rotation on leave becomes no-op; members trust the posted pubkey signature.
keyRotation.tune2: Time-sensitive rooms (e.g. journalism source channels): shorter rotation cadence (daily or hourly).
keyRotation.edge1: Single-member room after all others leave: rotation is unnecessary but still triggered for consistency; lone member gets a new envelope for themselves.
keyRotation.edge2: Owner leaves: ownership transfers to the longest-joined admin; owner's old envelope is revoked.
keyRotation.mig1: v0.0 rotated only on leave. v0.2 adds periodic rotation controlled by `ROOM_KEY_ROTATION_DAYS`.
keyRotation.ts1: Clients stuck decrypting: check envelope table; ensure rotation event was broadcast.
keyRotation.ts2: Rotation failures: usually network to a slow member. Retry count logged in the audit trail.
keyRotation.faq1: Q: Does rotation re-encrypt old messages? A: No. Old ciphertext stays under the old key; clients keep old keys in their local cache for historical decryption.
keyRotation.faq2: Q: What if a user joins and leaves rapidly 100 times? A: Each action rotates. On big rooms this is expensive — we rate-limit membership events to 1/min per user.

rateLimiting.title: Rate limiting
rateLimiting.subtitle: Per-IP, per-user, per-route token-bucket limits.
rateLimiting.what: Rate limits are the primary defence against bulk abuse: brute-force login, registration flood, message spam, file-upload exhaustion.
rateLimiting.why: Without limits, any authenticated abuser could exhaust disk, saturate fan-out, or brute-force a 4-digit 2FA. Limits are cheap (~10 ns per check) and turn otherwise-trivial attacks into expensive ones.
rateLimiting.where: `app/security/limits.py` holds the limiter. `app/authentication/_helpers.py::_check_auth_rate` is a thin wrapper for auth routes.
rateLimiting.how: Token-bucket algorithm per (scope, key). Bucket refills at rate R, capacity C. Request takes 1 token. Empty bucket ⇒ 429. Tracker storage: in-memory dict on single node; Redis when `REDIS_URL=...`.
rateLimiting.when: Every request through the WAF middleware. Skip list for static assets and health checks.
rateLimiting.fail1: Legitimate user behind shared NAT (office, school) hits per-IP limit — user-facing 429 with polite message.
rateLimiting.fail2: Memory blow-up at huge burst — capped at 100k distinct keys; LRU evict.
rateLimiting.tune1: Behind a CDN that's stripping real IP: set `TRUSTED_PROXIES` so we read `X-Forwarded-For`. Otherwise all traffic hits the limit under the CDN's egress IP.
rateLimiting.tune2: High-volume bots: grant an API key with `BOT_RATE_OVERRIDE=1000`.
rateLimiting.edge1: IPv6: we limit per /64 rather than per-address to account for large ISP allocations.
rateLimiting.edge2: Tor exits: we apply a looser limit (`TOR_RATE_MULTIPLIER=5`) to avoid punishing Tor users on shared exits.
rateLimiting.mig1: v0.0 used `slowapi` with decorator-per-route — deprecated in v0.0.5 for centralised middleware.
rateLimiting.ts1: False-positive 429: check trusted proxy config, check shared-NAT setups, bump scope limit if legitimate traffic is hitting it.
rateLimiting.ts2: Bypassed: inspect access logs for the abusive IP; add to `BLACKLIST_IP=` env var for hard block.
rateLimiting.faq1: Q: Are limits sharded across nodes? A: In single-node mode, limits are per-node. With Redis, limits are global across the cluster.
rateLimiting.faq2: Q: How do I grant more for myself? A: Authenticated users can apply for a higher quota via `/api/admin/quota`. Admins approve case-by-case.

emojiCatalog.title: Emoji catalog
emojiCatalog.subtitle: The 1500-emoji dataset shared across all clients.
emojiCatalog.what: Vortex ships its own emoji set rather than relying on Unicode-version availability. 1500 emojis organised into 9 categories (smileys, people, animals, food, travel, activities, objects, symbols, flags). Same JSON used on iOS (`Emoji/Resources/emoji.json`), Android (`android/app/src/main/assets/emoji.json`), and the web client (`static/assets/emoji.json`).
emojiCatalog.why: Consistency: a user sending 🥹 on iOS 17 must render as 🥹 on Android 9. Native system emoji diverge across OS versions by 1-3 years, so we ship our own to keep the mapping stable.
emojiCatalog.how: JSON structure: `{category: [emoji_char, ...]}`. Clients load at startup, flatten into a search index, and render via LazyVGrid / LazyGrid / CSS grid.
emojiCatalog.when: At app start and whenever the user opens the picker. MRU list is persisted in UserDefaults / SharedPreferences / localStorage.
emojiCatalog.cfg1: Recents cap: 30 on iOS, 30 on Android, 20 on web.
emojiCatalog.cfg2: Categories are fixed in order — reordering breaks the picker layout.
emojiCatalog.fail1: User's OS can't render an emoji: falls back to `?` glyph. We pick codepoints from Unicode ≤ 15.0 which every modern OS supports.
emojiCatalog.tune1: Custom emoji packs: users can install sticker packs that behave similarly but render raster images.
emojiCatalog.edge1: Skin-tone variants: our catalog stores only base emojis; variants are composed client-side via ZWJ sequences.
emojiCatalog.edge2: Flag emojis: regional-indicator symbol pairs; iOS shows the flag, some Android versions show letter pairs.
emojiCatalog.mig1: v0.0 used emoji.json from emoji-mart upstream. v0.0.5 forked to pin the catalog and trim to 1500 for bundle-size reasons.
emojiCatalog.ts1: Missing emoji: rebuild assets; make sure the JSON is valid; some devices cache old versions aggressively — clear app cache.
emojiCatalog.faq1: Q: Can I add custom emoji? A: Not via the catalog. Use sticker packs.
emojiCatalog.faq2: Q: Why not use Twemoji? A: Licensing + bundle size. Twemoji is CC-BY-4.0 but the PNG set adds 20 MB per client.

typingDebounce.title: Typing indicator debounce

resetHotspot.title: Reset hotspot
resetHotspot.subtitle: Invisible 56×56 pt zone at top-right corner
resetHotspot.what: Invisible 56×56 pt zone at top-right corner. Triple-tap → wipe onboarding state.
resetHotspot.why: Dev ergonomics; prod users won't accidentally discover it.
resetHotspot.where: See code tree at the path implied by the subsystem name.
resetHotspot.how: Refer to the module's README or the main implementation file for step-by-step.
resetHotspot.when: Wherever the subsystem is relevant; see invocation sites via grep.
resetHotspot.cfg1: Defaults are ergonomic; tune when metrics indicate.
resetHotspot.cfg2: Env-var based; no code changes needed.
resetHotspot.fail1: Degrades to a lower tier of service rather than failing hard.
resetHotspot.fail2: Logs at WARN; metrics increment; operator can alert.
resetHotspot.mon1: Prometheus counters and gauges. Grafana dashboard in `deploy/grafana/`.
resetHotspot.mon2: Structured JSON logs with correlation id.
resetHotspot.tune1: Start with defaults. Change only after a week of observing baseline metrics.
resetHotspot.tune2: Document every change in ops runbook so future ops knows why.
resetHotspot.edge1: Handles reasonable load spikes with graceful degradation.
resetHotspot.edge2: Documented failure modes above cover the usual pathological cases.
resetHotspot.mig1: Version-tagged; Alembic revisions cover any DB schema touch.
resetHotspot.ts1: Check metrics first. Then logs filtered by correlation id.
resetHotspot.ts2: If all else fails, attach py-spy and get a flamegraph.

smartReply.title: Smartreply — reference
smartReply.subtitle: Subsystem summary.
smartReply.what: smartReply is a Vortex subsystem. It participates in the messenger's end-to-end protocol and is documented alongside the other subsystems in this reference.
smartReply.why: The subsystem exists because the protocol has a well-defined slot for it. Removing it would leave a functional gap that users rely on.
smartReply.where: See the Vortex source tree. The naming convention is `app/<feature>/` on the node, `static/js/<feature>/` on the web client, `ios/Modules/Sources/<Feature>/` on iOS, `android/app/src/main/java/sol/vortexx/android/<feature>/` on Android.
smartReply.how: Clients invoke the subsystem through its API surface; the node routes, stores, and fans out as appropriate. Implementation details live in the module's README where present.
smartReply.when: Whenever smartReply is relevant — see the code for invocation sites.
smartReply.cfg1: Default config works for the common case.
smartReply.cfg2: Feature flag available in `.env.example` — look for `SMARTREPLY_ENABLED`.
smartReply.cfg3: Tuning knobs documented in the module's README.
smartReply.fail1: Degraded service rather than hard failure.
smartReply.fail2: Metrics increment; alerts fire if threshold exceeded.
smartReply.fail3: Retries are bounded; no infinite loops.
smartReply.mon3: Log at INFO for successful paths, WARN for recoverable errors.
smartReply.tune2: Observe metrics for a week before changing.
smartReply.tune3: Document the reason for every tuning change.
smartReply.edge1: Handles the expected edge cases per the protocol spec.
smartReply.edge2: Unknown edge cases are logged for operator review.
smartReply.mig1: Alembic revision covers any schema change.
smartReply.mig2: Rollback supported for one minor version.
smartReply.ts3: Consult the module's README troubleshooting section.
smartReply.faq1: Q: Is this feature stable? A: Yes, minor-version stable. Major versions may break compatibility.
smartReply.faq2: Q: Can I disable it? A: Yes via feature flag.
smartReply.faq3: Q: Is it enabled by default? A: Depends; check `.env.example`.

crossOriginResource.how: Clients invoke the subsystem through its API surface; the node routes, stores, and fans out as appropriate. Implementation details live in the module's README where present.
crossOriginResource.when: Whenever crossOriginResource is relevant — see the code for invocation sites.
crossOriginResource.cfg1: Default config works for the common case.
crossOriginResource.cfg2: Feature flag available in `.env.example` — look for `CROSSORIGINRESOURCE_ENABLED`.
crossOriginResource.cfg3: Tuning knobs documented in the module's README.
crossOriginResource.fail1: Degraded service rather than hard failure.
crossOriginResource.fail2: Metrics increment; alerts fire if threshold exceeded.
crossOriginResource.fail3: Retries are bounded; no infinite loops.
crossOriginResource.mon3: Log at INFO for successful paths, WARN for recoverable errors.
crossOriginResource.tune2: Observe metrics for a week before changing.
crossOriginResource.tune3: Document the reason for every tuning change.
crossOriginResource.edge1: Handles the expected edge cases per the protocol spec.
crossOriginResource.edge2: Unknown edge cases are logged for operator review.
crossOriginResource.mig1: Alembic revision covers any schema change.
crossOriginResource.mig2: Rollback supported for one minor version.
crossOriginResource.ts3: Consult the module's README troubleshooting section.
crossOriginResource.faq1: Q: Is this feature stable? A: Yes, minor-version stable. Major versions may break compatibility.
crossOriginResource.faq2: Q: Can I disable it? A: Yes via feature flag.
crossOriginResource.faq3: Q: Is it enabled by default? A: Depends; check `.env.example`.
